title: Classification Baseline Checklist
concept: Compare your deep recurrent classifier against a simple Naive Bayes baseline to make sure the LSTM is worth the compute. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Sequence Labeling.'

exercises.prompt: In machine learning, what is a 'baseline'?

options.text: The absolute best score a model can ever achieve.
A simple model used as a starting point to compare performance.
The final layer of a recurrent neural network.

exercises.explain: A baseline is a simple model that serves as a reference point to measure how much better (or worse) your advanced model performs.

cards.title: Do You Really Need That Big Model?
cards.body: Deep learning models are incredibly powerful, but they are also slow and expensive. How do we know if we actually need a super-smart neural network, or if a simple statistical trick from the 1950s can do the job just as well?

cards.title: What is a Baseline?
cards.body: A baseline is a simple, fast, and easy-to-run model (like Naive Bayes) that sets the minimum acceptable performance. If your complex LSTM model cannot beat the baseline, it is not worth the extra training time and compute power.

cards.title: The Monster Truck Test
cards.body: Imagine renting a giant, expensive monster truck just to drive down a perfectly flat, paved driveway. That is like using a complex, heavy LSTM model without checking if a lightweight bicycle—a Naive Bayes classifier—could do the exact same job in a fraction of a second!

cards.title: Saving Compute in the Real World
cards.body:
A team built a deep recurrent model to classify spam emails, which took 3 hours to train and scored 91% accuracy. 

When they ran a simple Naive Bayes baseline, it took only 2 seconds to train and scored 90.5% accuracy! The baseline showed that the complex model wasn't worth the extra resource cost.

cards.title: Myth: Complex is Always Better
cards.body: Many believe that deeper, more complex models are always better for text classification. In reality, simple algorithms like Naive Bayes often get very close to deep learning performance on basic text tasks, without needing a graphics card (GPU) to run!

cards.title: Baseline vs. Recurrent Model
cards.body: Compare the trade-offs between your baseline and your deep recurrent neural network.

right.text: Takes hours to train, requires expensive hardware, but understands complex sequences.

left.text: Trains in seconds, costs almost nothing, easy to deploy, but ignores word order.

exercises.prompt: Why is Naive Bayes commonly used as a baseline for text classification?

options.text: It is extremely fast to train and acts as a simple benchmark.
It automatically learns word order and grammar rules.
It requires specialized GPU hardware to run.
It is much more complex and slower than an LSTM.

exercises.explain: Naive Bayes is extremely fast to train and serve, making it the perfect benchmark to test if a slower LSTM is actually worth using.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If a complex LSTM model gets the exact same accuracy as a simple Naive Bayes baseline, you should still deploy the LSTM.
exercises.explain: If a simple baseline performs just as well, you should stick with it to save time, money, and compute power.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to complete the rule of thumb for ML models.
exercises.sentence: Always build a simple baseline first to justify using a complex neural network.
exercises.explain: A baseline model sets a standard point of comparison so you can prove your advanced model is actually bringing value.

exercises.prompt: Identify which machine learning workflow statement is incorrect or highly inefficient:

options.text: I will start with a simple Naive Bayes model to set a baseline score.
I will start by building a 10-layer recurrent network to establish my baseline.
I will compare my neural network's accuracy directly against my Naive Bayes score.

exercises.explain: Model B is the AI-generated or incorrect claim. In machine learning, we always want to start with the simplest baseline first, not the most complex deep neural network.

exercises.prompt: You have built a state-of-the-art LSTM text classifier. What is your next logical step to ensure this complex model is actually worth using?

options.text: Always use the deepest LSTM possible because more layers guarantee higher accuracy.
Train a simple Naive Bayes model first, evaluate it, and only keep the LSTM if it beats the baseline by a meaningful margin.
Delete the Naive Bayes code immediately so it doesn't slow down the training of the LSTM.
Use Naive Bayes to generate fake text, then feed that text into the LSTM to double the dataset size.

exercises.explain: Spot on! If Naive Bayes performs almost as well as your heavy LSTM, you probably don't need the extra weight and cost. Next up: Sequence Labeling.
